title: Thermoelectric information engine driven by an autonomous Maxwell demon across quantum-to-classical transitions
authors: Maximiliano Bernal Santibañez, Felipe Barra, Jose Mondaca
abstract: We study a three-terminal thermoelectric engine, focusing on the role of quantum coherence and information flow. A double-dot connects two reservoirs at different chemical potentials, while a third dot monitors their occupation via Coulomb interaction and can be interpreted as an autonomous Maxwell demon. Within the parameter range where the device operates as an engine, we identify conditions under which this interpretation holds. The system dynamics is described within a Redfield master equation that allows us to identify two distinct dynamical regimes with steady states well captured by suitable Lindblad approximations. These two regimes define a first quantum-to-classical transition controlled by the interdot tunneling strength. We further consider the effect of a phonon bath coupled to the double-dot, which induces a second quantum-to-classical transition by generating incoherent transport and decoherence in the small interdot tunneling regime. We identify a parameter region where phonon-induced decoherence suppresses both the coherent transport contribution and the information flow toward the monitoring dot, suggesting that coherence can enhance the demon mechanism in this regime. By tracking information and transport properties across these crossovers, our model shows how coherent tunneling, decoherence, and incoherent phonon-assisted transport compete in an autonomous information engine, while clarifying which thermodynamic Lindblad description is appropriate in each regime.
summary: The paper models a three-dot thermoelectric engine acting as an information processor driven by a Maxwell demon mechanism. It rigorously analyzes the system dynamics using multiple master equation approximations to map out different quantum-to-classical transition regimes. The findings clarify the competition between coherent tunneling, decoherence from phonons, and incoherent transport in enabling energy conversion via information flow.
main_problem: The study investigates a thermoelectric information engine driven by an autonomous Maxwell demon across different quantum-to-classical transition regimes.
main_result: The model demonstrates that the system's operation as an information engine is maintained across varying coupling strengths, with coherence and decoherence playing competing roles in determining transport mechanisms.
method: The dynamics are analyzed using open quantum system formalisms, comparing results from Redfield master equations against two distinct Lindblad approximations (global vs. semilocal) to determine the appropriate theoretical description for different parameter regimes.
model_or_system: The physical model is a three-terminal thermoelectric engine comprising two working dots (L and R) coupled to a monitoring 'Maxwell demon' dot (D). The system interacts with fermionic reservoirs and an explicit phonon bath, leading to complex non-equilibrium dynamics.
key_observables: Information flow ($\dot{I}$), energy/heat/work flows ($\dot{E}, \dot{Q}, \dot{W}$), coherence measures ($C_{l1}$), and the rate of entropy production ($\dot{\sigma}$).
important_parameters: The ratio of interdot tunneling strength to bath coupling ($g/\kappa_L$) controls the crossover between dynamical regimes, while phonon coupling strength ($J_0$) drives decoherence.
main_assumptions: Key approximations include the Born-Markov limit and the use of secular/partial-secular expansions for the master equation; spinless electrons are assumed.
figures_summary: Figures compare coherence and particle currents across parameter variations ($g/\kappa_L$ or $J_0$), illustrating how different theoretical approaches (Redfield, global, semilocal) predict transport properties in various regimes.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the system dynamics by deriving and comparing multiple master equation formalisms based on approximations related to frequency degeneracies. It then applies these tools to calculate thermodynamic quantities like work extraction and information flow across parameter sweeps that induce quantum-to-classical crossovers.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it merges concepts from open quantum systems, non-equilibrium thermodynamics (Maxwell's demon), and quantum transport, providing a rigorous framework for understanding how coherence affects energy/information conversion in nanoscale devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scheme of the system composed of three quan- tum dots (thick black lines), coupled via Coulomb interac- tions ULR and U, indicated by capacitors, and via coherent tunneling (g) in the case of the left (ϵL) and right (ϵR) quan- tum dots. Each site is coupled to a different bath. The left and right dots constitute the working substance and are also coupled to the phonon bath (green). Furthermore, quantum dot D (ϵD), coupled to an independent bath (orange), acts as the Maxwell demon through its interaction with the working substance.

FIG. 2. Coherence in the eigen basis Cl1(eig), coherence in the local basis Cl1(loc), and particle current, for two different values of phononic coupling J0/(βphκL) = 10−3 and J0/(βphκL) = 5 × 10−1. Results are shown for the partial master equation (red), global master equation (blue), and Redfield equation(black dashed). Cl1(eig) (a)-(b), Cl1(loc) (c)-(d) and particle current ˙NBL(e)-(f) all as a function of g/κL. Parameters: κL(ϵ) = κR(ϵ + U) = 1/100, κL(ϵ + U) = κR(ϵ) = 1/600, κL/κR = 6, κD/κL = 2, T/κL = 10000, TBD/κD = 100, µL = −µR, eV/T = 1, ULR/T = 5, U/TBD = 20, βph = βBL = βBR, µBD = 2 and sites energies ϵ = 0, ϵD = µBD −U/2 .
FIG. 3. (a) Energy flow, Power, contribution of information, free energy rate, heat flow by the working substance. (b) Energy flow and power ( ˙W1 = 0 =⇒ ˙E1 = ˙Q1), by the demon.

FIG. 4. Coherence Cl1(eig) versus the hopping parameter g/κL, for different values of the phonon coupling strength J0/(βphκL). Parameters: All parameters are the same of Fig. 2, except for J0/(βphκL).

FIG. 5. (a) Absolute value of the particle current | ˙NBL|/κL versus phonon coupling J0/(βphκL), for several parameters of coherent hopping g/κL. (b) ⟨˙Ncohe⟩/κL against J0/(βphκL). (c) Information flow received by the demon ˙I1 versus phonon coupling J0/(βphκL). (d) Efficiency of the working substance η2 against J0/(βphκL). Parameters: All parameters are the same of Fig. 2, except for g/κL and J0/(βphκL).
